skill.name: bio-ontology-mapper
skill.description: Maps unstructured biomedical text to standardized medical ontologies such as SNOMED CT and MeSH for semantic interoperability.
skill.summary: Converts free-text clinical and scientific terms into standardized ontology codes using local reference files and NIH API lookups.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: After evaluating 194 static findings, all are false positives caused by generic security patterns matching medical ontology data (MeSH tree numbers flagged as IP addresses, medical terms flagged as C2 keywords, markdown code blocks flagged as shell execution). The skill is a legitimate biomedical ontology mapper that accesses official NIH APIs (UMLS, MeSH) and reads API keys from environment variables. No malicious intent, data exfiltration, or prompt injection detected. Published as medium risk due to external network access and credential handling.

medium_findings.title: API Key Accessed from Environment Variable
medium_findings.description: The skill reads UMLS_API_KEY from environment variables and accepts it via CLI argument for authenticating with official NIH UMLS API. This is legitimate and expected for a biomedical ontology mapper that requires API authentication. No exfiltration was detected.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: API key access is real and confirmed by code review, but usage is legitimate - key is only sent to official NIH UMLS API endpoints with no exfiltration paths.

medium_findings.title: External Network Requests to Biomedical APIs
medium_findings.description: The skill makes HTTP requests to official NIH public APIs (UMLS UTS at uts-ws.nlm.nih.gov and MeSH at id.nlm.nih.gov). These are legitimate lookups for ontology mapping. Network access is expected for this skill type but should be documented for users concerned about data privacy.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Network activity is confirmed in the code, but it targets only official NIH endpoints. The skill properly warns users about PHI exposure risks in its documentation.

low_findings.title: MeSH Tree Numbers Misidentified as Hardcoded IP Addresses
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 49 MeSH tree classification codes (e.g. C14.280.647, C08.127.108) as hardcoded IP addresses. These are standard MeSH ontology tree numbers used for medical concept classification, not network addresses. FALSE POSITIVE.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Confirmed by reading mesh_sample.json - all flagged values are MeSH tree numbers like C14.280.647, not IP addresses.

low_findings.title: Medical Terms Misidentified as C2 Keywords
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 22 medical terms in reference data (cancer, cough, etc.) as potential C2 command-and-control keywords. These are legitimate biomedical ontology entries in a medical reference file. FALSE POSITIVE.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All flagged terms are standard medical terms in a MeSH ontology reference file. No command-and-control infrastructure present.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Blocks Misidentified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 55 markdown code blocks in SKILL.md as backtick shell execution. These are documentation examples showing bash commands, not active execution. FALSE POSITIVE.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: All flagged content is within markdown code fences showing example usage commands for documentation purposes only.

low_findings.title: MeSH Tree Numbers Misidentified as Cryptographic Algorithms
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 13 instances of MeSH tree numbers and lookup method names as weak cryptographic algorithms. No cryptographic operations exist in the codebase. FALSE POSITIVE.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Code review confirms no MD5, SHA1, base64, or any cryptographic operations exist. Flagged patterns are MeSH search method names and tree number values.

low_findings.title: Ontology Lookup Terms Misidentified as System Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 6 occurrences of words like lookup and search as system reconnaissance. These are legitimate biomedical ontology lookup operations. FALSE POSITIVE.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Context confirms these are ontology search/lookup method names, not system enumeration or reconnaissance commands.

low_findings.title: PHI Exposure Risk via External API Calls
low_findings.description: If user-provided biomedical text contains protected health information, sending it to external NIH APIs could pose a privacy risk. The skill documentation responsibly warns about this: 'De-identify before API calls; use local processing when possible'.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill has legitimate API integration but users should de-identify data before sending to external endpoints. The skill itself correctly documents this risk.

content.user_title: Map Biomedical Text to Standard Ontologies
content.value_statement: Researchers and clinicians spend significant time normalizing biomedical terminology across different data sources. This skill automatically maps free-text clinical terms to standardized ontologies including SNOMED CT and MeSH using both local reference matching and NIH API lookups.
actual_capabilities: Maps free-text biomedical terms to SNOMED CT concept IDs using local reference data and UMLS API lookup
Maps free-text biomedical terms to MeSH descriptor IDs using local reference data and MeSH API lookup
Expands abbreviations and synonyms using a built-in synonym dictionary for improved matching accuracy
Performs fuzzy string matching with configurable confidence thresholds to handle typos and variations
Supports batch processing of multiple terms from a text file with progress reporting
Outputs results in JSON or plain text format with confidence scores and match sources
limitations: Requires internet access and a UMLS API key for API-based lookups, which may not be available in all environments
Local reference data is limited to common medical conditions and may not cover rare diseases or new concepts
Fuzzy matching is based on string similarity only and does not use semantic or embedding-based approaches
Accuracy depends on the quality of input text; highly ambiguous or misspelled terms may produce incorrect mappings

use_cases.title: Clinical Research Data Harmonization
use_cases.description: Normalize free-text diagnosis descriptions from multiple clinical trial sites into standardized SNOMED CT codes for unified analysis.
use_cases.target_user: Clinical data manager harmonizing multi-site research data

use_cases.title: EHR Term Standardization
use_cases.description: Map unstructured clinical notes and problem lists from electronic health records to standardized ontology codes for interoperability.
use_cases.target_user: Health informatics specialist preparing EHR data for exchange

use_cases.title: Literature Indexing and Curation
use_cases.description: Convert free-text biomedical concepts from research papers into MeSH descriptors for consistent literature database indexing.
use_cases.target_user: Medical librarian or literature curator building searchable databases

prompt_templates.title: Map a Single Medical Term
prompt_templates.prompt: Map the biomedical term '{term}' to SNOMED CT and MeSH ontologies with a confidence threshold of {threshold}. Show the concept ID, preferred term, and confidence score for each match.
prompt_templates.scenario: Quick lookup of a single clinical term

prompt_templates.title: Batch Process Medical Terms from a File
prompt_templates.prompt: Process the file at '{input_path}' containing one biomedical term per line. Map each term to both SNOMED CT and MeSH ontologies. Write the results to '{output_path}' in JSON format and summarize the number of successful and failed mappings.
prompt_templates.scenario: Batch normalization of a list of clinical terms

prompt_templates.title: Cross-Ontology Translation
prompt_templates.prompt: Map the term '{term}' to SNOMED CT and then translate the SNOMED concept IDs to ICD-10 codes. Explain any mappings where the cross-ontology translation has low confidence.
prompt_templates.scenario: Translating clinical concepts between coding systems

prompt_templates.title: Validate and Audit Term Mappings
prompt_templates.prompt: Map the following biomedical terms to SNOMED CT: {terms}. For each mapping, identify terms with confidence below {threshold} and explain why the match may be uncertain. Suggest alternative concepts that could be considered for low-confidence mappings.
prompt_templates.scenario: Quality assurance review of ontology mappings

output_examples.input: Map the term 'heart attack' to SNOMED CT and MeSH
output_examples.output:
Found 2 mappings for 'heart attack':
- SNOMED CT: 22298006 (Myocardial infarction) - confidence 0.85
- MeSH: D009203 (Myocardial Infarction) - confidence 0.82
Local reference match used for both ontologies. API lookup was available.

output_examples.input: Batch map 'MI', 'HTN', and 'COPD' from the terms file
output_examples.output:
Processed 3 terms with 2 API lookups and 1 local match:
- MI: SNOMED CT 22298006 (Myocardial infarction) from API, confidence 0.91
- HTN: SNOMED CT 38341003 (Hypertensive disorder) from API, confidence 0.88
- COPD: SNOMED CT 13645005 (Chronic obstructive lung disease) from local match, confidence 0.76

best_practices: De-identify all patient data before sending terms to external APIs for ontology lookup to maintain HIPAA compliance
Set the confidence threshold to at least 0.7 for clinical applications and always review low-confidence matches manually
Use the local reference matching mode when working offline or with sensitive data that cannot be sent to external services
anti_patterns: Do not rely solely on API lookups for time-critical clinical decision support; API availability and latency cannot be guaranteed
Do not use raw string matching as the only validation step for high-stakes mappings; always involve domain expert review for patient care decisions
Do not assume ontology mappings remain valid over time; ontology versions change and mappings should be periodically revalidated

faq.question: What ontologies does this skill support?
faq.answer: The skill supports SNOMED CT and MeSH with local reference data. It can also access UMLS which includes ICD-10, LOINC, RxNorm, and HGNC through the NIH API.

faq.question: Do I need an API key to use this skill?
faq.answer: You need a free UMLS API key from the NIH for API-based lookups. The skill falls back to local matching if no API key is provided.

faq.question: Does this skill send my data to external servers?
faq.answer: Yes, when API mode is enabled, terms are sent to official NIH UMLS and MeSH APIs. You can disable API mode with --use-api=false for local-only matching.

faq.question: How accurate are the ontology mappings?
faq.answer: Accuracy depends on input quality. API lookups provide high accuracy for standard terms. Local fuzzy matching is less reliable for rare or ambiguous terms.

faq.question: Can this skill process protected health information?
faq.answer: You should de-identify all PHI before using API mode. Local matching mode can process PHI safely since no data leaves your environment.

faq.question: How do I interpret the confidence score?
faq.answer: Confidence scores range from 0.0 to 1.0. Scores above 0.7 indicate good matches. Scores below 0.7 should be reviewed manually for correctness.
